solution: 
```cpp
class MinStack {
private:
    stack<pair<int, int>> s;
public:
    MinStack() {
        
    }
    
    void push(int val) {
        if (s.empty()) {
            s.push({val, val});
        } else {
            s.push({val, min(val, s.top().second)});
        }
    }
    
    void pop() {
        s.pop();
    }
    
    int top() {
        return s.top().first;
    }
    
    int getMin() {
        return s.top().second;
    }
};

/**
 * Your MinStack object will be instantiated and called as such:
 * MinStack* obj = new MinStack();
 * obj->push(val);
 * obj->pop();
 * int param_3 = obj->top();
 * int param_4 = obj->getMin();
 */
```
efficiency: HIGH
time complexity: O(1)
space complexity: O(n)
energy implications: LOW
explanation: The solution uses a stack of pairs where each element stores the current value and the minimum value up to that point. This allows all operations to be performed in constant time. The key strength is the O(1) time complexity for all operations, while the weakness is the increased space usage due to storing two values per stack element.
